cards.back: Four genetically unique haploid (n) gametes from one diploid (2n) parent cell. One round of DNA replication, two rounds of division.

**What**: exchange of DNA between non-sister chromatids of homologous chromosomes at chiasmata.
**When**: prophase I.
**Why**: generates new allele combinations (genetic recombination).

**Anaphase I** separates homologous chromosomes (sister chromatids stay together).
**Anaphase II** separates sister chromatids.
In metaphase I, each homologous pair orients randomly relative to other pairs. With 23 pairs in humans, 2^23 ≈ 8.4 million combinations per gamete (before crossing over).
**XX** = female, **XY** = male. The Y chromosome carries the SRY gene that triggers male development. 22 pairs of autosomes are the same in both sexes.

**Meiosis I**: ALL FOUR gametes abnormal (2 extra, 2 missing).
**Meiosis II**: 2 normal + 2 abnormal (1 extra, 1 missing). Trisomy 21 → Down syndrome.

Human haploid (n) = 23 chromosomes (gametes).
Human diploid (2n) = 46 chromosomes (somatic cells).
Generates genetic diversity → at least some offspring survive when conditions change (disease, climate, predators). Asexual = clones, vulnerable to a single threat.

**Sister chromatids** = identical copies of one chromosome (made in S phase), joined at centromere.
**Homologous chromosomes** = matching pair (one maternal, one paternal); same genes, possibly different alleles.

cards.front: Compare metaphase I, II, and mitotic metaphase
cards.back:
**Met I**: homologous PAIRS at equator (random orientation = independent assortment).
**Met II** & **mitotic met**: single duplicated chromosomes lined up — but met II starts haploid.

**Genotype**: the two alleles for a gene (e.g. YY, Yy, yy).
**Phenotype**: the observable trait (yellow seed, green seed). Same phenotype can come from different genotypes.

**Homozygous**: two identical alleles (YY or yy).
**Heterozygous**: one of each (Yy). Heterozygotes can be carriers — unaffected but able to pass a recessive disease allele.
The two alleles for a gene separate during meiosis, so each gamete gets only one. Cellular basis: anaphase I separates homologous chromosomes. Heterozygote (Aa) → 1:1 gametes.
Alleles for one gene segregate into gametes independently of alleles for another (different) gene. Cellular basis: random orientation of pairs at metaphase I.
An organism whose self-fertilized offspring all show the same trait. True-breeding individuals are HOMOZYGOUS for the gene.

**P**: parental (true-breeding).
**F1**: first filial — hybrid offspring of P. From YY×yy, all F1 = Yy (heterozygous, dominant phenotype).
**F2**: F1 self-cross → 3:1 dominant:recessive.

**Incomplete**: heterozygote shows BLENDED intermediate (red × white → pink).
**Codominance**: BOTH phenotypes appear simultaneously (I^A I^B → type AB blood, both A and B antigens).

**Polygenic**: ONE trait controlled by MANY genes (e.g. skin color, height) — continuous variation.
**Pleiotropy**: ONE gene with MANY unrelated effects (e.g. Marfan syndrome → tall stature + lens dislocation + aortic aneurysm).

cards.front: X-linked recessive — why males more affected?
cards.back: Females (XX) need two copies to express. Males (XY) have only one X — any recessive X-linked allele is expressed because Y carries no copy. Males CANNOT be carriers.

In each cell of an XX individual, ONE X is randomly inactivated → Barr body. Prevents double-dosing of X-linked gene products. Why female calicos have patchy fur and why Rett syndrome severity varies in girls.

cards.front: Three components of a DNA nucleotide
cards.back:
1. Phosphate group (PO₄⁻)
2. Deoxyribose sugar (5-carbon)
3. Nitrogenous base (A, T, G, or C)
The sugar-phosphate backbone faces the EXTERIOR; bases face the INTERIOR.

**A pairs with T** via 2 hydrogen bonds.
**G pairs with C** via 3 hydrogen bonds.
In RNA, **U replaces T** (so A pairs with U).
%A = %T and %G = %C in any double-stranded DNA. So if T = 20%, then A = 20%, G + C = 60% → G = C = 30%.
The two DNA strands run in opposite 5' → 3' directions. This is why one new strand is leading (continuous) and the other is lagging (Okazaki fragments).

**H**elicase: unwinds helix.
**S**SB: stabilizes single strands.
**L**(topoiso) topoisomerase: relieves tension ahead of fork.
**P**rimase: lays RNA primer.
**P**ol III: extends DNA 5'→3' (proofreads).
**P**ol I: replaces RNA primers with DNA.
**L**igase: seals gaps.

**Leading**: continuous, toward replication fork, one primer.
**Lagging**: away from fork, in Okazaki fragments, multiple primers. Both reasons stem from DNA pol III's 5'→3' rule + antiparallel template.
Each daughter helix has ONE original (template) strand and ONE newly synthesized strand. Confirmed by the Meselson-Stahl experiment (1958).
After the last RNA primer is removed from the lagging strand, no upstream 3'-OH exists for DNA pol to extend — the end gap can't fill. Each replication clips a small piece off linear chromosomes.

cards.front: Telomerase — what it does and where
cards.back: Adds repetitive DNA (TTAGGG) to the 3' end of telomeres using its own RNA template. Active in **germ cells, stem cells, ~85% of cancers**. Inactive in most adult somatic cells.

cards.front: S phase, the cell cycle, and replication
cards.back: DNA replicates ONCE during **S (synthesis) phase** of interphase. Cell cycle: G1 → S → G2 → M (mitosis or meiosis). Same mechanism whether mitosis or meiosis follows.

**DNA → (transcription) → RNA → (translation) → Protein**.
Transcription happens in the nucleus by RNA polymerase. Translation happens at ribosomes in the cytoplasm.

1. **Sugar**: ribose (RNA) vs. deoxyribose (DNA).
2. **Base**: U in RNA replaces T.
3. **Strands**: RNA single-stranded; DNA double-stranded helix.

**mRNA**: messenger — carries the gene's message to the ribosome.
**tRNA**: transfer — adaptor, brings amino acids to the ribosome (anticodon ↔ codon).
**rRNA**: ribosomal — structural + catalytic component of the ribosome (a ribozyme).

**Initiation**: RNA polymerase binds the promoter (with transcription factors in eukaryotes), unwinds DNA.
**Elongation**: builds RNA 5'→3' from template strand.
**Termination**: at terminator sequence, everything dissociates.

1. **5' cap** (modified G) — ribosome recognition, stability.
2. **3' poly-A tail** (~100-250 A's) — stability, export.
3. **Splicing** — introns removed, exons joined. Alternative splicing → multiple proteins from one gene.

**Initiation**: small subunit + initiator tRNA(Met) bind 5' cap, scan to AUG; large subunit joins.
**Elongation**: tRNA enters A site, peptide bond forms (catalyzed by rRNA), ribosome shifts.
**Termination**: stop codon → release factor → polypeptide released.

**Start**: AUG → methionine. Every protein starts with Met.
**Stop**: UAA, UAG, UGA → no amino acid; release factor binds. Mnemonic: U Are Away · U Are Gone · U Go Away.
A 3-nucleotide sequence on mRNA that specifies one amino acid. 64 codons total: 61 code for amino acids, 3 are stop codons. The genetic code is REDUNDANT (most amino acids have multiple codons).

**Substitution**: silent / missense / nonsense.
**Insertion** or **Deletion**: in-frame = mild; not multiple of 3 = **frameshift** (every downstream codon changed; usually catastrophic).
Classic example: **sickle cell** = single A→T substitution (Glu → Val).

1. Chromatin packing (histones)
2. Transcription factors
3. Alternative splicing
4. mRNA export from nucleus
5. mRNA stability + microRNA silencing
6. Post-translational (folding, modification, degradation)
Polymerase Chain Reaction: amplifies a SPECIFIC DNA sequence exponentially. Each cycle DOUBLES the target. After n cycles → 2ⁿ copies. Invented by Kary Mullis (Nobel 1993).

1. **Target DNA** (template)
2. **Heat-stable DNA polymerase** (Taq)
3. **dNTPs** (free nucleotides)
4. **Primers** (two short DNAs flanking the target)

1. **Denaturation @ ~95°C** — H-bonds break, strands separate
2. **Annealing @ ~55°C** — primers bind to single strands
3. **Extension @ ~72°C** — Taq polymerase synthesizes new DNA from primers
The 95°C denaturation step would destroy ordinary DNA polymerase. Taq polymerase from *Thermus aquaticus* (a hot-spring bacterium) survives the cycling temperatures.
DNA is negatively charged (phosphate backbone) → migrates toward + electrode. SMALLER fragments slip through agarose mesh faster, traveling FARTHER in the same time. Result: bands by size.
A short DNA sequence (2-6 bp) repeated head-to-tail many times. The NUMBER of repeats varies between individuals. CODIS uses 13 STR loci → 1-in-a-trillion uniqueness for DNA profiling.

Replicate the unknown DNA with normal nucleotides + dye-labeled chain-terminator nucleotides. Each position produces fragments of one length, color-coded by terminal base. Run on a gel and read sequence.

**Biotechnology**: any use of organisms or biomolecules for human purposes (PCR, vaccines, GMO, fingerprinting).
**Genetic engineering**: SUBSET — direct alteration of an organism's genome (recombinant DNA, CRISPR).
Joining DNA from different sources → e.g. inserting human insulin gene into a bacterial plasmid lets the bacterium produce human insulin cheaply at scale.
Sequenced ALL 3 billion human DNA bases by 2003 (started 1990). Found ~19,949 genes (far fewer than predicted). Genes are only ~2% of the genome — most DNA is non-coding.

**Mitosis**: 2 identical diploid cells (growth/repair).
**Meiosis**: 4 unique haploid cells (gametes for sexual reproduction).

**Transcription**: DNA → RNA, in nucleus, by RNA polymerase.
**Translation**: mRNA → protein, in cytoplasm at ribosomes, using tRNA adaptors.

Replication: copies ALL DNA, makes DNA, uses DNA pol III, RNA primer needed, creates 2 identical helices.
Transcription: copies ONE gene, makes RNA, uses RNA pol, no primer, creates 1 RNA strand.

**Prokaryotes**: single circular chromosome, ONE origin, often + plasmids, fast (~1000 nt/s).
**Eukaryotes**: multiple linear chromosomes, MANY origins, slower (~50 nt/s) but parallelized.

**Leading**: continuous, toward fork, ONE primer.
**Lagging**: discontinuous, away from fork, MULTIPLE primers (one per Okazaki fragment), needs DNA pol I + ligase to finish.

cards.front: Incomplete dominance vs. codominance — visualize blood
cards.back:
**Incomplete**: blend (red + white → pink). One blended phenotype.
**Codominance**: both visible (A-antigen + B-antigen → type AB blood). Two distinct phenotypes simultaneously.

**Sister chromatids**: identical copies of ONE chromosome (formed in S phase, joined at centromere).
**Homologues**: same chromosome NUMBER but different ORIGINS (one maternal, one paternal); same genes, possibly different alleles.

cards.front: Anaphase I vs. anaphase II vs. mitotic anaphase
cards.back:
**Ana I**: separates HOMOLOGUES (chromatids stay together).
**Ana II** & **mitotic**: separate SISTER CHROMATIDS. Identical mechanism; ana II just starts haploid.

**Ex**ons stay = **EX**pressed (in mature mRNA, code for protein).
**In**trons go = **IN**tervening (spliced out, never reach the ribosome).
Alternative splicing of exons → multiple proteins from one gene.

**Substitution** (point): one base swapped. Effect ranges from silent (codon redundancy) to missense (sickle cell) to nonsense (premature stop).
**Frameshift**: insertion/deletion not divisible by 3 → reading frame shifts → ALL downstream codons change → usually catastrophic.
